subsystem: TensorFlow Lite kernel shape-inference helper: ResizeOutputTensors for the SPLIT_V operator. Given an input tensor, a size_splits tensor, and an axis tensor, it computes each output tensor's shape by distributing the input's size along `axis` among the outputs, resolving an optional single -1 ('infer this split') entry, and then calls ResizeTensor for every output.
provenance: Identified from TFLite-specific types and idioms: TfLiteStatus/TfLiteContext/TfLiteNode/TfLiteTensor, GetTensorData<int>(), NumDimensions()/SizeOfDimension()/NumOutputs(), TfLiteIntArray/TfLiteIntArrayCopy, GetOutputSafe(), the TF_LITE_ENSURE/TF_LITE_ENSURE_OK/TF_LITE_ENSURE_STATUS macros, kTfLiteInt32/kTfLiteInt64/kTfLiteOk/kTfLiteError enums, and context->ReportError/context->ResizeTensor. The function name and size_splits/-1 semantics match tensorflow/lite/kernels/split_v.cc. The sample banner 'primevul_train_2117_fixed' and filename train_0011_cwe125.cpp signal this is a patched variant whose historical bug class is an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125).
trust_boundary: The tensors input, size_splits, and axis are model-supplied: a .tflite model (or its serialized flatbuffer) is attacker-controllable in any deployment that loads untrusted models. Their element counts, dtypes, and every scalar value (axis[0], each size_split, NumDimensions/SizeOfDimension of input, and NumOutputs(node)) are hostile inputs. Nothing here re-validates that these were checked in the operator's Prepare phase, so this function must treat axis[0], the split values, and the output count as adversarial.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function computes and applies output shapes for SPLIT_V and returns a TfLiteStatus. `context` is the interpreter context used for error reporting and for the ResizeTensor callback; `node` carries the operator's output list; `input` is the tensor being split; `size_splits` is a 1-D tensor of per-output split sizes (int32 or int64), one of which may be -1 to mean 'infer'; `axis` is a scalar tensor naming the dimension of `input` to split along. All three data tensors originate from the loaded model and are therefore untrusted.

It reads the first element of the axis tensor as a signed int. If the axis is negative (Python-style indexing from the end), it normalizes by adding the input rank NumDimensions(input). Note this read and normalization happen BEFORE any validation of how many elements `axis` actually has, and before the range checks at lines 37-38. axis_value can still be negative after normalization if the original value was more negative than -rank.

It materializes the split sizes into a std::vector<int64_t>. Depending on the size_splits tensor dtype it dispatches to the templated GetSizeSplitsVector, which copies the tensor's elements (widening int32 to int64) into size_splits_vector. Any other dtype is rejected with an error and an immediate return kTfLiteError. This is the only branch in the function that actually returns on an error condition. The vector contents are fully attacker-controlled numbers.

Two accumulators: minus_one_index remembers the index of the single split entry equal to -1 (the 'infer size' marker), initialized to the sentinel -1 meaning 'none seen yet'; size_splits_sum accumulates the sum of all NON-(-1) split sizes so the inferred split can later be computed as input_size - size_splits_sum.

A single pass over the split values. The first -1 records its index. A second -1 triggers an error message but nothing else — control keeps iterating and, crucially, the function does NOT return. Every non-(-1) value is added to size_splits_sum. The loop counter is a signed int compared against the unsigned size_splits_vector.size(). After this loop, size_splits_sum excludes ALL -1 entries, and minus_one_index points only at the FIRST -1.

These two TF_LITE_ENSURE macros are the guard that a fixed version adds: they force axis_value into the valid range [0, NumDimensions(input)) and bail out with kTfLiteError otherwise. Only after both pass is SizeOfDimension(input, axis_value) read, so the dimension access is now in bounds. input_size is the length of the input along the split axis, and the -1 split (if any) must fill the remainder up to this value.

Resolves the inferred split. If a -1 was present and the other splits do not already exceed input_size, the -1 slot is replaced by the remainder input_size - size_splits_sum (guaranteed >= 0 by the size_splits_sum > input_size check). If the sum overflows input_size, it reports an error but does NOT return. If there was no -1, the splits must exactly sum to input_size; otherwise it reports an error but again does NOT return. In both error cases execution falls through to the resize loop below.

For each of the node's outputs it clones the input's dimension array (TfLiteIntArrayCopy allocates a fresh TfLiteIntArray), overwrites the split axis with the i-th split size (using the bounds-checked .at(i)), fetches the i-th output tensor via GetOutputSafe, and calls ResizeTensor to apply the new shape. The loop bound is NumOutputs(node), which is NOT compared against size_splits_vector.size(); the split value written into output_dims->data[axis_value] can be negative if an unresolved -1 survived from the fall-through error paths above.

On completing the loop the function reports success. Because several earlier error paths (multiple -1, sum-too-large, sum-mismatch) only log and fall through, kTfLiteOk can be returned even after those 'errors', so a malformed size_splits still produces resized (possibly invalid) outputs.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The 'more than one -1' case only logs; there is no return kTfLiteError. minus_one_index keeps pointing at the FIRST -1, and every additional -1 stays as the literal value -1 in size_splits_vector because those entries are skipped by the summation branch. At line 47 only the first -1 slot is replaced with input_size - size_splits_sum; the remaining -1 slots are never fixed up. In the resize loop (line 57) those outputs get output_dims->data[axis_value] = -1, a negative dimension handed to ResizeTensor. TFLite computes a tensor's byte size as a product of its dimensions, so a negative axis length either underflows the size computation to a huge value (over-allocation / subsequent out-of-bounds writes when the tensor is populated) or corrupts downstream shape logic. The invariant the implementation must uphold: a second -1 must return kTfLiteError before any output is resized.

When a -1 is present but the other splits already exceed input_size, the code logs an error and, because there is no return, skips the else assignment at line 47 that would have replaced the -1 slot. The -1 therefore survives into the resize loop, and output_dims->data[axis_value] = -1 is written for that output (line 57). Same failure mode as above: a negative dimension reaches ResizeTensor. The safe contract is 'reject (return kTfLiteError) before the resize loop when size_splits_sum > input_size', not merely log.

GetTensorData<int>(axis)[0] dereferences element 0 of the axis tensor with no check that axis has at least one element. A model that supplies a zero-element (empty) axis tensor causes an out-of-bounds read of the tensor's backing buffer (CWE-125) before any validation runs. It also assumes axis is int32; if the model declares axis as int64, the reinterpretation reads only the low 4 bytes, yielding a wrong axis. The implementation must confirm NumElements(axis) >= 1 (and the expected dtype) — typically in Prepare — before this read; nothing in this function guarantees it.

The loop iterates i over NumOutputs(node) but indexes size_splits_vector.at(i). There is no check that NumOutputs(node) == size_splits_vector.size(). If the model declares more outputs than there are split entries, .at(i) throws std::out_of_range; in a TFLite build compiled with -fno-exceptions this becomes std::terminate/abort, i.e. a remotely triggerable denial of service. The invariant: the number of outputs must equal the number of size_splits before this loop; the function should TF_LITE_ENSURE that equality rather than rely on .at to trap.

With no -1 present, the splits must sum exactly to input_size. On mismatch the code logs but does not return, so it proceeds to resize the outputs using split sizes that do not partition the axis. The resulting output shapes are internally inconsistent with the actual SPLIT_V computation performed later, and the op can then read or write past the intended per-slice boundaries when the split is executed. The safe behavior is to return kTfLiteError on the sum mismatch.

The loop compares a signed int i against the unsigned std::vector::size_type returned by size(). For a size_splits tensor with more than INT_MAX elements the comparison forces i's promotion and the counter overflows to a negative value on increment (signed overflow is undefined behavior), producing a potential infinite loop or misindexing. This is low-risk given practical tensor sizes but is a real sign-conversion hygiene defect; the counter should be size_t (or the size cast explicitly).

TfLiteIntArrayCopy allocates a fresh array each iteration. If GetOutputSafe (line 59) or ResizeTensor (line 60) fails, the TF_LITE_ENSURE_OK/TF_LITE_ENSURE_STATUS macros return early WITHOUT freeing output_dims that was just allocated on line 56, leaking that allocation (CWE-401). Likewise if .at(i) throws on line 57 the allocation leaks. Low severity, but the ownership contract for output_dims on the error paths is not upheld here.

These checks are the mitigation for the historical SPLIT_V out-of-bounds read (CWE-125): an unvalidated axis_value would drive SizeOfDimension(input, axis_value) at line 39 and output_dims->data[axis_value] at line 57 out of the dims array. Here they correctly gate BOTH of those accesses, so that specific OOB is closed. The residual concern is ordering discipline: the axis[0] read at line 6 and the axis_value += NumDimensions(input) normalization at line 8 execute before these guards, so any future access to axis-derived state added above line 37 would again be unguarded. Confirm no such use is introduced and that the guards remain ahead of every axis-indexed access.

summary: This is the SPLIT_V ResizeOutputTensors shape helper; the historical unchecked-axis out-of-bounds read is now closed by the axis range guards at lines 37-38. However, three validation error paths (multiple -1 at 28-31, sum-too-large at 42-45, sum-mismatch at 49-52) only log and fall through without returning, so a malformed model-supplied size_splits can leave a -1 (negative) dimension that reaches ResizeTensor, and the output loop trusts NumOutputs(node) against size_splits without an equality check. The strongest residual risks are model-controlled negative output dimensions (improper-validation -> bad allocation) and an empty-axis out-of-bounds read at line 6.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: ResizeOutputTensors(TfLiteContext*, TfLiteNode*, const TfLiteTensor* input, const TfLiteTensor* size_splits, const TfLiteTensor* axis) signature
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; each untrusted parameter is reviewed at its point of use below.
Element 0 dereferenced with no NumElements(axis)>=1 or dtype check — empty/wrong-typed axis tensor yields OOB read.
Result is range-checked by the TF_LITE_ENSURE guards at lines 37-38 before any dimension-indexed use.
Unsupported dtype is rejected with an immediate return kTfLiteError (lines 17-18); values themselves flow into the -1/negative-dimension findings.
Container populated from the tensor; its dangerous contents are covered by the -1-handling and NumOutputs-mismatch findings.
Local scalars; safe in isolation, but their downstream use drives the missing-return findings.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: loop bound i < size_splits_vector.size() with int i (line 24)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Signed/unsigned comparison and signed counter overflow for very large tensors.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: 'more than one -1' error branch without return (lines 28-31)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Logs but does not return; extra -1 entries survive as negative output dimensions.
.at is bounds-checked and int64 accumulation of tensor values has ample range; overflow not reachable with realistic split counts.
This is the fix: it gates the axis-indexed accesses at lines 39 and 57 and closes the historical CWE-125; noted as mitigation in the walkthrough.
axis_value validated in-range by the preceding ensures, so this dimension read is bounded.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: size_splits_sum > input_size error branch without return (lines 42-45)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Logs but does not return; the -1 slot is left unresolved and reaches ResizeTensor as a negative dimension.
minus_one_index is a valid index and the size_splits_sum > input_size guard makes the subtraction non-negative on this path.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: size_splits_sum != input_size error branch without return (lines 49-52)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Logs but does not return; outputs are resized with splits that do not partition the axis.
No equality check between NumOutputs(node) and size_splits_vector.size(); .at throws/aborts on mismatch (DoS).
Allocation leaks on the early-return macro paths (lines 59-60) and on a thrown .at (line 57).
axis_value index is guarded, but the assigned value can be a leftover -1 (negative dimension) — folded into the missing-return findings.
Safe output accessor with checked status via TF_LITE_ENSURE_OK.
Status is checked; its danger is receiving a negative dimension, which is attributed to the upstream missing-return findings.
Success return; its reachability after logged-but-not-returned errors is captured by the missing-return findings.

findings.title: Second -1 in size_splits logged but not rejected -> negative output dimension reaches ResizeTensor
findings.anchor: 'more than one -1' else branch (context->ReportError without return)
findings.bug_class: Non-enforcing validation -> negative tensor dimension / improper size computation
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0011_cwe125.cpp (and the SPLIT_V ResizeTensor implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: Encountering a second -1 in size_splits must return kTfLiteError before any output is resized; only one inferred split is permitted.
findings.failure_mode: A model supplying size_splits with two or more -1 entries logs an error but keeps executing. Only the first -1 is replaced (line 47); the remaining -1 values stay in size_splits_vector and are written as output_dims->data[axis_value] = -1 at line 57. ResizeTensor then receives a negative axis length, so the tensor byte-size product underflows to a huge value (over-allocation and later out-of-bounds writes when the slice is filled) or corrupts shape state. Reachable purely from an untrusted .tflite model.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the else branch returns kTfLiteError (as upstream TFLite does) rather than only calling ReportError, and that no output can be resized with a residual -1 dimension.

findings.title: size_splits_sum > input_size logged but not rejected -> unresolved -1 dimension
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0011_cwe125.cpp (and the SPLIT_V ResizeTensor implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: When a -1 is present and the remaining splits already exceed input_size, the function must return kTfLiteError before resizing; the inferred split can never be filled validly.
findings.failure_mode: With a -1 present and size_splits_sum > input_size, the code logs and falls through, skipping the else assignment at line 47. The -1 slot is never replaced, so output_dims->data[axis_value] = -1 (line 57) hands ResizeTensor a negative dimension, with the same over-allocation / out-of-bounds consequences as the multiple-(-1) case. Attacker-controlled via crafted size_splits values.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm this branch returns kTfLiteError before the resize loop, and that a -1 index cannot survive unresolved into line 57.

findings.title: axis[0] dereferenced without element-count / dtype check (out-of-bounds read)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0011_cwe125.cpp (guarantee expected in the SPLIT_V Prepare, file not provided)
findings.invariant: axis must have at least one element and the expected int32 dtype before element 0 is read.
findings.failure_mode: A model providing a zero-element axis tensor causes GetTensorData<int>(axis)[0] to read past the (empty) buffer — an out-of-bounds read of whatever backs the tensor. A wrong dtype (int64) is silently reinterpreted as int32, selecting an incorrect axis. This read runs before any validation in this function, so safety depends entirely on a Prepare-time check that is not visible here.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the operator's Prepare (or this function) enforces NumElements(axis) >= 1 and axis->type == kTfLiteInt32 before line 6.

findings.title: Output count trusted against size_splits length -> .at() throws / aborts
findings.bug_class: Unhandled exception / reachable abort (denial of service)
findings.invariant: NumOutputs(node) must equal size_splits_vector.size() before iterating; the output count and split count must agree.
findings.failure_mode: The loop runs i up to NumOutputs(node) but indexes size_splits_vector.at(i) with no equality check. A model declaring more outputs than split entries makes .at(i) throw std::out_of_range; under TFLite's typical -fno-exceptions build this is std::terminate/abort, a remotely triggerable crash. (It also leaks the output_dims allocated on line 56 on that path.)
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm a TF_LITE_ENSURE_EQ(NumOutputs(node), size_splits_vector.size()) exists here or in Prepare before the loop.

findings.title: size_splits sum mismatch logged but not rejected -> resize with invalid partition
findings.anchor: else if (size_splits_sum != input_size) context->ReportError(...) without return
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0011_cwe125.cpp (and the SPLIT_V compute kernel, file not provided)
findings.invariant: When no -1 is present, the splits must sum exactly to input_size or the function must return kTfLiteError before resizing.
findings.failure_mode: On a sum mismatch with no -1, the code logs and continues, resizing outputs with split sizes that do not partition the axis. The declared output shapes then disagree with the actual data the SPLIT_V compute step slices, enabling out-of-bounds reads/writes during execution and returning kTfLiteOk for a malformed configuration.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm this branch returns kTfLiteError, matching upstream TFLite behavior.

findings.title: output_dims leaked on early-return error paths
findings.anchor: TfLiteIntArrayCopy(input->dims) not freed before TF_LITE_ENSURE_OK/STATUS returns
findings.invariant: Every TfLiteIntArrayCopy result must be freed or ownership-transferred on all control-flow paths, including the ensure-macro early returns.
findings.failure_mode: If GetOutputSafe fails (line 59) or ResizeTensor fails (line 60), the enclosing TF_LITE_ENSURE_OK/TF_LITE_ENSURE_STATUS returns immediately, and the output_dims array allocated on line 56 is never freed. Repeated failures leak memory; a thrown .at(i) on line 57 leaks similarly.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm ResizeTensor takes ownership of output_dims and that the GetOutputSafe/exception paths free it (or restructure to free before the ensure returns).

findings.title: Signed loop counter compared against unsigned size()
findings.bug_class: Signed/unsigned comparison and signed counter overflow
findings.invariant: The loop index type must hold every valid vector index (use size_t) so the comparison is well-defined for all sizes.
findings.failure_mode: For a size_splits_vector with more than INT_MAX elements, the signed int counter overflows on increment (undefined behavior), potentially looping forever or misindexing. Practically bounded by tensor sizes but a genuine sign-hygiene defect.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the counter is size_t (or the size is cast) and that split counts are otherwise capped upstream.

audit_checklist: Verify the 'more than one -1' branch (lines 28-31) returns kTfLiteError before any output is resized; confirm no residual -1 can reach output_dims->data[axis_value] at line 57.
Verify the size_splits_sum > input_size branch (lines 42-45) returns kTfLiteError so the inferred -1 slot is never left unresolved.
Verify the size_splits_sum != input_size branch (lines 49-52) returns kTfLiteError rather than resizing with a non-partitioning split set.
Confirm axis is validated (NumElements(axis) >= 1 and axis->type == kTfLiteInt32) before GetTensorData<int>(axis)[0] on line 6, in this function or in Prepare.
Confirm NumOutputs(node) equals size_splits_vector.size() before the resize loop (line 55) to prevent .at(i) from throwing/aborting.
Confirm ResizeTensor rejects (or is never reached with) negative or zero-collapsed axis dimensions.
Confirm output_dims allocated by TfLiteIntArrayCopy (line 56) is freed on the GetOutputSafe/ResizeTensor early-return and exception paths.
Confirm the axis range guards (lines 37-38) remain positioned ahead of every axis_value-indexed access (lines 39, 57) so the historical CWE-125 stays closed.
Change the loop counter on line 24 to size_t (or cast size()) to remove the signed/unsigned comparison and overflow risk.
